card.intuition: Per-parameter adaptive rates by accumulating all past squared gradients: Gₜ = Σ_{τ=1}^{t} gτ². Update: θ ← θ − (α/√(Gₜ+ε))·gₜ. Rarely updated parameters get large effective rates; frequently updated ones get small rates — ideal for sparse features (NLP). Drawback: G only grows → learning rate monotonically decreases to zero.
card.analogy: Studying for exams: topics you've spent a lot of time on (large accumulated gradient) get smaller additional study sessions (smaller effective rate); unfamiliar topics (small accumulated gradient) get larger sessions. Eventually all topics get so much study time that additional sessions become negligibly small — the Adagrad stopping problem.
when_to_use: Sparse features and NLP tasks where most parameters update rarely
Convex problems where the decreasing rate is a feature not a bug
Conceptually simpler than Adam — good for teaching adaptive methods
gotchas: Monotonically decreasing rate kills learning in non-convex deep models — use RMSprop/Adam instead
Initial learning rate α needs careful tuning — 0.01 is a common default
GloVe embeddings were originally trained with Adagrad

exercise.starter_code:
import math

def adagrad_step(params, grads, G, lr=0.01, eps=1e-8):
    """
    params, grads: lists of floats.
    G: accumulated sum of squared gradients (modified in place).
    """
    for i in range(len(params)):
        G[i] += grads[i] ** 2
        params[i] -= ___________
    return params, G
hints: Adagrad update: θ -= lr · g / (√G + ε) where G accumulates all past squared gradients.
exercise.test_code:
import math
params = [3.0]; G = [0.0]
for _ in range(500):
    g = [2.0 * params[0]]
    params, G = adagrad_step(params, g, G, lr=0.5)
assert abs(params[0]) < 0.2, f'Should converge: {params[0]:.4f}'
assert G[0] > 0  # G monotonically grows
print('PASS')
